message:
fix(raptorq): correct misleading 0.02% overhead claim to accurate ~5%

The article previously cited "0.02%" as RaptorQ's overhead, which was
the reception overhead (K+2 symbols at K=10,000) — a cherry-picked
best-case metric that ignores the precode's ~2.5% internal expansion
from K source symbols to L ≈ 1.025K intermediate symbols.

This contradicted the article's own Composition Trick section which
correctly derives ~5% total overhead via K(1 + δ)(1 + ε') ≈ K(1.05).

Changes:
- Hero subtitle: "0.02%" → "under 5%"
- Intro paragraph: reworded to cite total overhead under 5% and the
  "+2 symbols" reception overhead separately
- "How Good Is It, Really?" callout: replaced misleading percentage
  with "K + 2" absolute figure, added new paragraph explaining the
  distinction between reception overhead and total system overhead
  (precode expansion + LT layer)

Sources: Shokrollahi 2006 (IEEE Trans. IT), RFC 6330, Qualcomm
RaptorQ Technical Overview, Ramly et al. 2022 (5G measurements).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -190,8 +190,8 @@ export function RaptorQArticle() {

Turn any file into an infinite stream of interchangeable packets. Collect any K of them, in any order, and recover the original. The - overhead: about{" "} - 0.02%. + total overhead: under{" "} + 5%.

@@ -216,9 +216,10 @@ export function RaptorQArticle() { The idea that you can take some arbitrary file, turn it into a stream of fungible blobs, receive those blobs in literally any order, and have each new one help you reconstruct the original already seems pretty - impressive. But then you learn that the overhead for all of this is - something like 0.02%, and that seems both magical and frankly - improbable. + impressive. But then you learn that the total overhead for all of + this is under 5%, and that the receiver often needs just two extra + symbols beyond the bare minimum to decode with near-certainty. + That seems both magical and frankly improbable.

To see why this matters, think about how we normally move data around. @@ -257,17 +258,18 @@ export function RaptorQArticle() { the average decode failure rate must be at most 1 in 100 when receiving symbols, 1 in 10,000 at{" "} , and 1 in 1,000,000 at . - To be clear about what that means: at , - two extra symbols is about{" "} - 0.02%{" "} - overhead. That’s it. + The receiver almost never needs more than{" "} + K + 2{" "} + symbols to decode perfectly.

- One caveat: that 0.02% figure is a function of block size. The - absolute overhead is "+2 symbols" regardless of , - so the percentage depends on how big your block is. - At , two extra symbols is 0.02%. - At , those same two symbols are 4%. + But "+2 symbols" is only the reception overhead—the + extra packets the receiver must collect. The full picture includes + the precode’s internal expansion from source symbols + to intermediate symbols. That ~2.5% + structural redundancy is what makes the "+2 symbols" trick + possible. Combined with the LT layer, total system overhead is + under 5%—still remarkably small.
